stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \). The matrix \( A \) is invertible. What is the entry in the first row and first column of \( A^{-1} \)?
solution: For \( A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} \), \( A^{-1} = \frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix} \). With \( ad-bc = ([[a]])([[d]])-([[b]])([[c]]) \), the \( (1,1) \) entry is \( \frac{[[d]]}{([[a]])([[d]])-([[b]])([[c]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This puts \( a \) in the \( (1,1) \) slot; the adjugate places \( d \) there (the diagonal entries are swapped).
This uses \( bc-ad \) in the denominator, flipping the sign of the determinant.
This forgets to divide by the determinant \( ad-bc \).
This uses the entry \( b \) rather than \( d \) in the numerator.
This uses the entry \( c \) rather than \( d \) in the numerator.
This has the wrong sign in the numerator.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} k & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), where \( k \) is a real parameter. For which value of \( k \) does \( A^{-1} \) fail to exist?
solution: \( A^{-1} \) exists exactly when \( \det(A) = [[d]]k - ([[b]])([[c]]) \ne 0 \). It fails to exist when \( [[d]]k = ([[b]])([[c]]) \), i.e. \( k = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the product \( bc \); one must still divide by \( [[d]] \) to isolate \( k \).
This solves the singularity equation with the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), so \( \det(A) = [[detA]] \) and \( A \) is invertible. What is \( \det(A^{-1}) \)?
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the reciprocal and just gives \( \det(A) \) back.
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This negates \( \det(A) \) instead of taking its reciprocal.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), so \( \det(A) = [[detA]] \) and \( A \) is invertible. What is \( \operatorname{tr}(A^{-1}) \)?
solution: Since \( A^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det A}\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix} \), its trace is \( \dfrac{a+d}{\det A} = \dfrac{[[a]]+[[d]]}{[[detA]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( \det(A) \), giving just \( \operatorname{tr}(A) \).
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This multiplies by \( \det(A) \) instead of dividing by it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
